title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: networks created equal. Many real-world networks bipartite, meaning two distinct types nodes, ties form types, never within . Students enroll courses. Countries sign treaties. Legislators join committees. Users purchase products. case, adjacency matrix rectangular rather square, row column nodes play fundamentally different roles. lame package supports bipartite networks ame() (cross-sectional) lame() (longitudinal). key difference unipartite case model uses separate latent spaces two node types, connected interaction matrix GG captures row-node column-node characteristics relate .

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: bipartite network, probability tie row node ii column node jj modeled : yij=β′xij+ai+bj+ui′Gvj+ϵijy_{ij} = \beta'x_{ij} + a_i + b_j + u_i'Gv_j + \epsilon_{ij} additive effects work just unipartite case: aia_i captures “active” row node ii (e.g., student takes many courses), bjb_j captures “popular” column node jj (e.g., course high enrollment). multiplicative term ui′Gvju_i'Gv_j different. Instead ui′vju_i'v_j shared latent dimensions, row column nodes live potentially different-dimensional latent spaces (RrowR_\text{row} RcolR_\text{col}), interaction matrix GG maps . flexible, letting model discover , say, two dimensions student preferences map onto three dimensions course characteristics. things drop away bipartite setting. dyadic correlation parameter ρ\rho, ties inherently directional row column nodes (can’t reciprocal tie student–course network). additive effects aa bb also independent variances rather joint covariance, since describe fundamentally different types actors.

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: illustrate workflow, simulate bipartite network 30 students 20 courses. student 2-dimensional latent position captures academic preferences, course 2-dimensional latent position captures kind student attracts. interaction matrix GG determines latent dimensions combine predict enrollment.
code: # simulate a student-course enrollment network n_students <- 30 n_courses <- 20  # true latent positions (unobserved in practice) U_true <- matrix(rnorm(n_students * 2, 0, 0.8), n_students, 2) V_true <- matrix(rnorm(n_courses * 2, 0, 0.8), n_courses, 2)  # interaction matrix: dimension 1 has positive affinity, # dimension 2 has negative (students high on dim 2 avoid courses high on dim 2) G_true <- matrix(c(1, 0.5, 0.5, -1), 2, 2)  # generate enrollment probabilities and binary outcomes # negative intercept keeps enrollment rate realistic (~25-30%) eta <- -0.8 + U_true %*% G_true %*% t(V_true) prob <- pnorm(eta) Y_bipartite <- matrix(rbinom(n_students * n_courses, 1, prob),                                             n_students, n_courses)  rownames(Y_bipartite) <- paste0("Student", 1:n_students) colnames(Y_bipartite) <- paste0("Course", 1:n_courses)  cat("Network dimensions:", dim(Y_bipartite), "\n") #> Network dimensions: 30 20 cat("Enrollment rate:", round(mean(Y_bipartite), 2), "\n") #> Enrollment rate: 0.29

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: Fitting bipartite model requires setting mode = "bipartite" specifying latent dimensions node type separately via R_row R_col. summary shows regression coefficient (just intercept , since included covariates) variance components. fitted object also stores student latent positions (U), course latent positions (V), interaction matrix (G), additive effects (APM student activity, BPM course popularity), ’ll visualize next.
code: # note: burn/nscan are kept small here for fast vignette building. # for real analyses, use burn >= 1000 and nscan >= 5000. fit_cross <- ame(     Y = Y_bipartite,     mode = "bipartite",     R_row = 2,              # latent dimensions for students     R_col = 2,              # latent dimensions for courses     family = "binary",     burn = 100,     nscan = 500,     odens = 5,     verbose = FALSE )  summary(fit_cross) #>  #> === AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ a[i] + b[j], family = 'binary'" #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>       Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper    #> beta0   -0.597    0.195  -3.062   0.002   -0.963   -0.221 ** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: p-values are approximate (posterior mean / SD); use credible intervals for inference. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.309    0.120 #> cab    0.000    0.000 #> vb     0.401    0.135 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #> rho    0.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) #>   Note: bipartite model (rho fixed to 0, cab fixed to 0)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: uv_plot function layout = "biplot" places row column nodes latent space, making easy see students cluster near courses. Students courses appear close together plot higher predicted probability tie.  interaction matrix GG mediates latent dimensions students courses combine predict ties. Keep mind GG, UU, VV identified rotation scaling: model can absorb rotations UU VV GG, individual entries GG stable interpretation across runs. identified overall product UGV′UGV', captures latent structure network. diagonal entries GG indicate overall strength latent factor association, -diagonal entries indicate cross-dimension interactions.
code: uv_plot(fit_cross, layout = "biplot") +     ggtitle("Bipartite Latent Space: Students and Courses") G_melt <- melt(fit_cross$G) ggplot(G_melt, aes(x = Var2, y = Var1, fill = value)) +     geom_tile() +     scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red") +     labs(title = "Estimated Interaction Matrix G",             x = "Column Dimension", y = "Row Dimension") +     theme_bw() +     theme(panel.border = element_blank())

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: bipartite networks observed multiple time periods (say, student enrollments across semesters, country–treaty memberships across decades), lame() lets pool information across time optionally allowing latent structure evolve.

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: simulate panel user–item interaction networks 5 periods. underlying latent structure held fixed (now), small random shocks overall baseline period.
code:
n_periods <- 5 n_users <- 20 n_items <- 15  U_long <- matrix(rnorm(n_users * 2), n_users, 2) V_long <- matrix(rnorm(n_items * 2), n_items, 2) G_long <- matrix(c(1, 0.3, 0.3, -0.8), 2, 2)  Y_list <- list() for(t in 1:n_periods) {     eta_t <- U_long %*% G_long %*% t(V_long) + rnorm(1, 0, 0.2)     prob_t <- pnorm(eta_t)     Y_list[[t]] <- matrix(rbinom(n_users * n_items, 1, prob_t),                                                 n_users, n_items)     rownames(Y_list[[t]]) <- paste0("User", 1:n_users)     colnames(Y_list[[t]]) <- paste0("Item", 1:n_items) } names(Y_list) <- paste0("T", 1:n_periods)  cat("Time periods:", length(Y_list), "
") #> Time periods: 5 cat("Dimensions per period:", dim(Y_list[[1]]), "
") #> Dimensions per period: 20 15 cat("Average density:", round(mean(sapply(Y_list, mean)), 2), "
") #> Average density: 0.52

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: simplest longitudinal model assumes latent positions additive effects constant time. pools time periods together get single estimate actor’s latent position, useful believe underlying structure stable just want data estimate precisely.
code: # note: iterations are kept small for vignette speed; use burn >= 1000 # and nscan >= 5000 for real analyses. fit_static <- lame(     Y = Y_list,     mode = "bipartite",     R_row = 2,     R_col = 2,     family = "binary",     dynamic_uv = FALSE,     dynamic_ab = FALSE,     burn = 100,     nscan = 500,     odens = 5,     verbose = FALSE,     plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_static) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> NULL #>  #> Time periods: 5  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: bipartite  #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper   #> intercept    0.056    0.159   0.354   0.723   -0.227    0.313   #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: p-values are approximate (posterior mean / SD); use credible intervals for inference. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.234    0.075 #> cab    0.000    0.000 #> vb     0.267    0.100 #> rho    0.000    0.000 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) #>   Note: bipartite model (rho fixed to 0, cab fixed to 0)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: suspect latent structure changes time (users’ tastes shift, items gain lose popularity), dynamic model lets latent positions additive effects drift via AR(1) processes. high autoregressive parameter (ρ\rho close 1) means slow, gradual change; low one means structure volatile period period. dynamic model reports estimated AR(1) persistence parameters latent positions (rho_uv) additive effects (rho_ab). Since simulated data uses latent structure across time periods, parameters reflect interplay prior (favors persistence) lack temporal variation data. real applications user preferences item popularity genuinely shift time, see parameters clearly informed data.
code: # same reduced iterations as above for vignette speed. fit_dynamic <- lame(     Y = Y_list,     mode = "bipartite",     R_row = 2,     R_col = 2,     family = "binary",     dynamic_uv = TRUE,     dynamic_ab = TRUE,     burn = 100,     nscan = 500,     odens = 5,     verbose = FALSE,     plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_dynamic) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> NULL #>  #> Time periods: 5  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: bipartite  #> Dynamic latent positions: enabled (rho_uv = 0 ) #> Dynamic additive effects: enabled (rho_ab = 0.485 ) #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper   #> intercept   -0.003    0.038   -0.07   0.944   -0.085    0.067   #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: p-values are approximate (posterior mean / SD); use credible intervals for inference. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.236    0.074 #> cab    0.000    0.000 #> vb     0.272    0.088 #> rho    0.000    0.000 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) #>   Note: bipartite model (rho fixed to 0, cab fixed to 0)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: dynamic effects, uv_plot can show trajectories, illustrating actor’s latent position moves space time. line traces one actor’s path first last period, revealing actors’ roles stable shifting.  Convergence diagnostics especially important dynamic models, since additional parameters (ρ\rho, innovation variances) need adequate samples well-estimated.
code: uv_plot(fit_dynamic, plot_type = "trajectory") +     ggtitle("Dynamic Latent Positions: Trajectories Over Time") trace_plot(fit_dynamic)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: natural question whether dynamic model worth added complexity. goodness--fit plots provide one way compare: dynamic model’s posterior predictive distributions better cover observed statistics, extra flexibility paying .   can also compare GOF statistics directly. bipartite networks, key statistics standard deviation row means (much users vary activity), standard deviation column means (much items vary popularity), four-cycle count (measure clustering bipartite networks, analogous transitivity unipartite networks).  Since simulated data fixed latent structure across time periods, static dynamic models fit similarly. boxplots overlap substantially cover dashed line, dynamic model’s extra flexibility paying . real applications user preferences item popularity genuinely shift time, dynamic model show tighter coverage observed statistics.
code: # GOF plots for each model gof_plot(fit_static) gof_plot(fit_dynamic) gof_static <- fit_static$GOF gof_dynamic <- fit_dynamic$GOF  # extract posterior predictive samples (exclude column 1, which is observed) # each element is a matrix [n_time x n_mcmc], so colMeans averages across time extract_ppc <- function(gof, stat) {     mat <- gof[[stat]]     colMeans(mat[, -1, drop = FALSE]) }  # observed values (column 1, averaged across time periods) obs_vals <- c(     mean(gof_static$sd.rowmean[, 1]),     mean(gof_static$sd.colmean[, 1]),     mean(gof_static$four.cycles[, 1]) )  gof_df <- data.frame(     value = c(         extract_ppc(gof_static, "sd.rowmean"),         extract_ppc(gof_dynamic, "sd.rowmean"),         extract_ppc(gof_static, "sd.colmean"),         extract_ppc(gof_dynamic, "sd.colmean"),         extract_ppc(gof_static, "four.cycles"),         extract_ppc(gof_dynamic, "four.cycles")     ),     model = rep(rep(c("Static", "Dynamic"),                             each = ncol(gof_static$sd.rowmean) - 1), 3),     statistic = rep(c("Row Mean SD", "Column Mean SD", "Four-Cycles"),                                     each = 2 * (ncol(gof_static$sd.rowmean) - 1)) )  obs_df <- data.frame(     statistic = c("Row Mean SD", "Column Mean SD", "Four-Cycles"),     observed = obs_vals )  ggplot(gof_df, aes(x = model, y = value)) +     geom_boxplot() +     geom_hline(data = obs_df, aes(yintercept = observed),                         linetype = 2) +     facet_wrap(~statistic, scales = "free_y") +     labs(title = "GOF Comparison: Static vs Dynamic",             subtitle = "Dashed Line = Observed Value",             x = "Model", y = "Simulated Statistic") +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         strip.background = element_rect(fill = "black", color = "black"),         strip.text = element_text(color = "white", hjust = 0)     )

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: number latent dimensions (R_row, R_col) controls rich multiplicative structure . dimensions model can’t capture latent clustering; many risk overfitting (slower computation). practical approach fit models different dimensions compare GOF statistics. posterior predictive p-values tell well model reproduces observed network structure. Values near 0.5 indicate good fit (observed statistic falls middle simulated distribution). Values near 0 1 indicate model consistently - -predicts feature. Look point adding dimensions stops improving p-values.
code: # include R=0 as a no-latent-space baseline dims_to_test <- list(     c(0, 0),     c(1, 1),     c(2, 2) )  # compute observed GOF statistics from the data obs_gof <- gof_stats(Y_bipartite, mode = "bipartite")  gof_results <- list() for(i in seq_along(dims_to_test)) {     fit_temp <- ame(         Y = Y_bipartite,         mode = "bipartite",         R_row = dims_to_test[[i]][1],         R_col = dims_to_test[[i]][2],         family = "binary",         burn = 200,         nscan = 2500,         odens = 5,         verbose = FALSE     )     # posterior predictive p-values: proportion of simulated     # statistics as or more extreme than the observed value     gof_results[[i]] <- c(         R_row = dims_to_test[[i]][1],         R_col = dims_to_test[[i]][2],         pval_rowmean = mean(fit_temp$GOF[, "sd.rowmean"] >= obs_gof["sd.rowmean"]),         pval_fourcycles = mean(fit_temp$GOF[, "four.cycles"] >= obs_gof["four.cycles"])     ) }  do.call(rbind, gof_results) #>      R_row R_col pval_rowmean pval_fourcycles #> [1,]     0     0     1.000000       0.9920160 #> [2,]     1     1     0.998004       0.9820359 #> [3,]     2     2     0.988024       0.9700599

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: use bipartite models. Use bipartite mode whenever network two fundamentally different types nodes ties form types. adjacency matrix rectangular, almost certainly need . Even happens square (e.g., 20 students 20 courses), set mode = "bipartite" row column nodes represent different kinds entities. Interpreting output. additive effects (APM, BPM) direct interpretation: tell row nodes unusually active column nodes unusually popular, controlling covariates latent structure. latent positions (U, V) interaction matrix (G) together capture residual patterns association, answering question “beyond covariates overall activity levels, row nodes tend connect column nodes?”

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: Bayesian model, always check convergence interpreting results. trace_plot function shows MCMC traces posterior densities regression coefficients variance components.  Look traces mix well (long flat stretches slow drifts) densities smooth unimodal. short chains used vignette, convergence guaranteed. practice, run longer chains consider multiple starting values.
code: trace_plot(fit_cross)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: need latent positions tidy format (e.g., merging external data building custom plots), latent_positions() function returns data frame one row per actor-dimension-time combination: especially useful bipartite networks U (row nodes) V (column nodes) different numbers actors.
code: lp <- latent_positions(fit_cross) head(lp) #>      actor dimension time       value posterior_sd type #> 1 Student1         1 <NA> -2.12110916           NA    U #> 2 Student2         1 <NA>  0.49495598           NA    U #> 3 Student3         1 <NA>  1.74106967           NA    U #> 4 Student4         1 <NA> -1.90187944           NA    U #> 5 Student5         1 <NA> -0.03972852           NA    U #> 6 Student6         1 <NA>  4.32579340           NA    U  # filter to just the row nodes (students) lp_students <- lp[lp$type == "U", ] cat("Student positions:", nrow(lp_students), "rows\n") #> Student positions: 60 rows

title: Your First AME Model
text: Imagine ’re studying friendships high school. data nominated friend, plus information student (gender, race, grade). natural first instinct run logistic regression: sharing gender predict friendship? problem friendships aren’t independent observations. students just social others (nominate lots friends regardless ). students popular (receive lots nominations). friendships tend reciprocated: Alice names Bob, Bob likely name Alice. standard regression ignores , standard errors wrong. Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) model handles dependencies directly. gives actor sender effect (aia_i, social ), receiver effect (bjb_j, popular ), position latent space (uiu_i, vjv_j) captures tends connect beyond covariates explain. name reflects two kinds actor-level terms: sender receiver effects enter model additively (ai+bja_i + b_j), latent positions enter multiplicatively (ui′vju_i'v_j, dot product). Think regression takes network structure seriously.

title: Your First AME Model
text: ’ll analyze friendship network Add Health study, longitudinal study adolescents United States. addhealthc3 dataset lame contains directed friendship nomination network along student characteristics. Notice na.rm = TRUE calls: network data often missing entries (diagonal NA self-ties undefined, dyads may unobserved). model handles missing values internally via data augmentation, need impute . Just leave NAs matrix pass directly. modeling, let’s look basic structure. much students vary number friends?  ’s real variation : students much social others, much popular. exactly sender receiver random effects capture.
code:
library(lame) library(ggplot2) set.seed(6886)  # load the Add Health friendship network data(addhealthc3)  # convert valued network to binary (any nomination = friendship) Y <- (addhealthc3$Y > 0) * 1 X_nodes <- addhealthc3$X  n <- nrow(Y) cat("Students:", n, "
") #> Students: 32 cat("Friendships:", sum(Y, na.rm = TRUE), "
") #> Friendships: 127 cat("Network density:", round(mean(Y, na.rm = TRUE), 3), "
") #> Network density: 0.128 out_degree <- rowSums(Y, na.rm = TRUE) in_degree <- colSums(Y, na.rm = TRUE)  degree_df <- data.frame(     Degree = c(out_degree, in_degree),     Type = rep(c("Nominations sent", "Nominations received"), each = n) )  ggplot(degree_df, aes(x = Degree)) +     geom_histogram(binwidth = 1) +     facet_wrap(~Type, ncol = 2) +     labs(x = "Number of Ties", y = "Count") +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         strip.background = element_rect(fill = "black", color = "black"),         strip.text = element_text(color = "white", hjust = 0)     )

title: Your First AME Model
text: classic question friendship networks homophily: birds feather flock together? ’ll test whether students likely friends others gender, race, grade. deliberately include same_grade (binary: grade ) grade_diff (continuous: many grades apart) illustrate common modeling pitfall: collinearity. two covariates measure nearly thing, model cleanly separate effects. ’ll see consequences results .
code: # homophily indicators: 1 if same, 0 if different same_female <- outer(X_nodes[,"female"], X_nodes[,"female"], "==") * 1 same_race <- outer(X_nodes[,"race"], X_nodes[,"race"], "==") * 1 same_grade <- outer(X_nodes[,"grade"], X_nodes[,"grade"], "==") * 1 grade_diff <- abs(outer(X_nodes[,"grade"], X_nodes[,"grade"], "-"))  # pack into a 3D array (n x n x p) Xdyad <- array(NA, dim = c(n, n, 4)) Xdyad[,,1] <- same_female Xdyad[,,2] <- same_race Xdyad[,,3] <- same_grade Xdyad[,,4] <- grade_diff dimnames(Xdyad)[[3]] <- c('same_female', 'same_race', 'same_grade', 'grade_diff') for(k in 1:4) diag(Xdyad[,,k]) <- NA  # nodal covariates (sender and receiver characteristics) Xrow <- X_nodes[, c("female", "grade")] Xcol <- X_nodes[, c("female", "grade")]

title: Your First AME Model
text: Now fun part. fit binary probit AME model : Covariates homophily effects (similar students become friends?) Sender receiver random effects (students social/popular) Dyadic correlation (reciprocity: friendships tend mutual) 2-dimensional latent space (residual clustering beyond covariates explain)
code: fit <- ame(Y,                     Xdyad = Xdyad,                     Xrow = Xrow,                     Xcol = Xcol,                     R = 2,              # 2D latent space                     family = "binary",  # probit model for 0/1 data                     rvar = TRUE,        # sender random effects                     cvar = TRUE,        # receiver random effects                     dcor = TRUE,        # dyadic correlation (reciprocity)                     burn = 500,         # burn-in (increase for publication)                     nscan = 2000,       # post-burn-in iterations                     odens = 25,         # thinning                     verbose = FALSE,                     gof = TRUE)

title: Your First AME Model
text: Let’s unpack key results: Homophily effects. Look regression coefficients credible intervals. grade_diff coefficient strongly negative credible interval excludes zero, telling us students apart grade much less likely friends. same_female coefficient positive (gender homophily), though credible interval may include zero depending run. same_race coefficient captures race homophily controlling grade gender. collinearity pitfall. Notice same_grade coefficient difficult interpret: sign magnitude unstable same_grade grade_diff nearly redundant (grade difference 0 equivalent grade). exactly included — show model runs without error coefficients become unreliable covariates collinear. practice, include one , . Variance components. sender variance (va) receiver variance (vb) quantify much students differ sociality popularity. Larger values mean heterogeneity. dyadic correlation (rho) captures reciprocity: values near 1 mean nominates B, B almost always nominates back. network, reciprocity strong. binary networks probit link, coefficients latent scale. rough rule thumb 0.4×β0.4 \times \beta approximates marginal probability change, holds baseline probability near 0.5. sparse networks (density 0.1, common international relations many social networks), marginal effect considerably smaller. get predicted probabilities specific dyads, use predict(fit, type = "response").
code: summary(fit) #>  #> === AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ intercept + dyad(same_female, same_race, same_grade, grade_diff) + row(female, grade) + col(female, grade) + a[i] + b[j] + rho*e[ji] + U[i,1:2] %*% V[j,1:2], family = 'binary'" #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>                  Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept          -2.523    0.677  -3.725       0   -3.809   -1.443 *** #> female_row         -0.347    0.254  -1.362   0.173   -0.752    0.122     #> grade_row           0.088    0.045   1.972   0.049    0.009    0.196   * #> female_col         -0.289    0.248  -1.165   0.244   -0.745    0.134     #> grade_col           0.135    0.039   3.424   0.001    0.051    0.207 *** #> same_female_dyad    0.305    0.167   1.826   0.068   -0.048    0.643   . #> same_race_dyad     -0.026    0.222  -0.115   0.908   -0.426    0.395     #> same_grade_dyad     0.074    0.231   0.322   0.747   -0.351    0.493     #> grade_diff_dyad    -0.563    0.089  -6.313       0   -0.721   -0.382 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: p-values are approximate (posterior mean / SD); use credible intervals for inference. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.323    0.106 #> cab    0.012    0.070 #> vb     0.206    0.076 #> rho    0.877    0.065 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance)

title: Your First AME Model
text: Since model estimated via MCMC (Markov chain Monte Carlo), need verify sampler explored posterior distribution thoroughly. trace_plot function first tool reach .  look : trace plots (top) look like “fuzzy caterpillars” bouncing around stable mean. see long trends, chain hasn’t converged. density plots (bottom) smooth unimodal. 80 post-burn-samples, convergence reasonable, publication ’d want longer chains.
code: trace_plot(fit, params = "beta", ncol = 3)

what: Does the Model Fit the Data?
title: Your First AME Model
text: Goodness--fit (GOF) checks whether model can reproduce key structural features observed network. goes beyond dyad-level prediction: tests whether model captures emergent properties like degree heterogeneity, reciprocity patterns, clustering.  panel shows different network statistic. histogram distribution simulated networks drawn fitted model; vertical line observed value. observed value falls well within simulated distribution, model capturing aspect data. falls tails, model missing something. common AME models fit degree heterogeneity reciprocity well (explicitly modeled) -predict transitivity cyclicity (require higher-order dependencies model directly capture). can also compute GOF fact using gof() function, useful want test custom statistics: model-expected value close observed value, model reproducing aspect network structure well. large gap suggests model missing something important. , observed degree correlation likely higher model expects, meaning students nominate many friends also tend receive many nominations. AME model captures sender-receiver covariance (cab), degree correlation also reflects higher-order structure. useful diagnostic: tells model well limitations, guiding decisions whether add covariates increase latent dimension.
code: gof_plot(fit) # define a custom statistic: degree correlation custom_stats <- function(Y) {     out_deg <- rowSums(Y, na.rm = TRUE)     in_deg <- colSums(Y, na.rm = TRUE)     c(deg_cor = cor(out_deg, in_deg)) }  gof_custom <- gof(fit, custom_gof = custom_stats, nsim = 50, verbose = FALSE) cat("Observed degree correlation:", round(gof_custom[1, "deg_cor"], 3), "\n") #> Observed degree correlation: 0.47 cat("Model expected:", round(mean(gof_custom[-1, "deg_cor"]), 3), "\n") #> Model expected: 0.264

title: Your First AME Model
text: multiplicative effects (ui′vju_i'v_j) place student 2D latent space. Students near sender space (triangles) tend nominate similar friends; students near receiver space (circles) tend nominated similar students. captures clustering covariates alone can’t explain.  Students opposite sides plot dissimilar friendship patterns. Clusters students placed near likely share unobserved characteristic (social group, extracurricular activities) drives friendship choices beyond gender, race, grade.
code: uv_plot(fit, layout = "circle", show.edges = FALSE,                 label.nodes = TRUE, label.size = 2.5) +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         axis.text = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(),         panel.grid = element_blank()     )

what: Who Are the Most Social / Popular Students?
title: Your First AME Model
text: additive effects decompose individual heterogeneity sender effects (sociality) receiver effects (popularity):  Students large positive sender effects nominate friends expected given covariates; large positive receiver effects receive nominations.
code: p1 <- ab_plot(fit, effect = "sender", sorted = TRUE,                             title = "Sender Effects (Sociality)") p2 <- ab_plot(fit, effect = "receiver", sorted = TRUE,                             title = "Receiver Effects (Popularity)")  library(patchwork) p1 / p2

title: Your First AME Model
text: model gives predicted probabilities every possible tie, including unobserved ones. useful link prediction (likely become friends?) understanding model’s fit dyad level. caveat: sparse networks (density 0.1), raw accuracy can misleadingly high predicting “tie” every dyad already gets predictions right. Focus -diagonal cells confusion matrix see well model distinguishes true ties non-ties. Confusion Matrix (threshold = 0.5) serious link prediction, consider area ROC curve (AUC), evaluates model’s ability rank true ties non-ties across thresholds rather relying single cutpoint.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: Networks change. Countries close allies decade ago may drifted apart. legislator’s co-sponsorship patterns shift gain seniority switch committee assignments. user’s purchasing habits evolve tastes change. standard AME model assumes actor fixed latent position fixed additive effects across time periods. reasonable starting point (pooling across time gives data precise estimates), can miss important dynamics. lame package provides two mechanisms letting model capture temporal change: dynamic_uv time-varying latent positions dynamic_ab time-varying additive effects. vignette explains options , use , interpret results.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: standard AME model longitudinal data, tie actors ii jj time tt : yij,t=β′xij,t+ai+bj+ui′vj+ϵij,ty_{ij,t} = \beta'x_{ij,t} + a_i + b_j + u_i'v_j + \epsilon_{ij,t} covariates (xij,tx_{ij,t}) can vary time, everything else (sender effect aia_i, receiver effect bjb_j, latent positions uiu_i vjv_j) constant. means model assumes country’s tendency sanction others, position latent “sanctioning space,” 1993 2000.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: set dynamic_uv = TRUE, actor’s latent position evolves time according AR(1) process: Ui,k,t=ρuvUi,k,t−1+ϵi,k,tU_{,k,t} = \rho_{uv} \, U_{,k,t-1} + \epsilon_{,k,t}Vj,k,t=ρuvVj,k,t−1+ηj,k,tV_{j,k,t} = \rho_{uv} \, V_{j,k,t-1} + \eta_{j,k,t} autoregressive parameter ρuv\rho_{uv} controls persistent positions . value close 1 means positions change slowly, last year’s position strong predictor year’s. value close 0 means positions essentially re-drawn period. practice, ρuv\rho_{uv} estimated data, don’t need choose . useful believe underlying community structure evolving: alliances shift, social groups re-form, trading blocs realign.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: set dynamic_ab = TRUE, sender receiver effects evolve time: ai,t=ρabai,t−1+ϵi,ta_{,t} = \rho_{ab} \, a_{,t-1} + \epsilon_{,t}bj,t=ρabbj,t−1+ηj,tb_{j,t} = \rho_{ab} \, b_{j,t-1} + \eta_{j,t} captures changes actors’ overall activity levels. country might become active sanctioner change government. student might become less socially active across semesters. changes much actor participates, connect (’s dynamic_uv captures). can use either option alone combine . practice, dynamic_ab often good place start, since changes overall activity common relatively easy estimate. dynamic_uv adds flexibility also parameters.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: AR(1) coefficients innovation variances sensible default priors: ρuv∼TruncNormal(0.9,0.1,0,1)\rho_{uv} \sim \text{TruncNormal}(0.9, 0.1, 0, 1), centered high persistence ρab∼TruncNormal(0.8,0.15,0,1)\rho_{ab} \sim \text{TruncNormal}(0.8, 0.15, 0, 1), slightly less persistent σuv2,σab2∼InverseGamma(2,1)\sigma_{uv}^2, \sigma_{ab}^2 \sim \text{InverseGamma}(2, 1) can customized via prior argument strong beliefs rate change.

what: Genuine Dynamics: What They Look Like
title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: start example dynamics truly present, can see model recovers real signal. simulate 25 actors 5 periods actor’s latent position evolves via AR(1) process ρ=0.85\rho = 0.85. use stronger latent positions (SD = 1.5) negative intercept produce network realistic density (roughly 30%). trajectory plot shows actors move latent space time. genuine temporal structure, paths show coherent movement rather random jumping:  Always check convergence. Dynamic models parameters static models, adequate mixing harder achieve requires longer chains:
code: library(lame) set.seed(6886)  n_dyn <- 25 n_per <- 5 R_dyn <- 2  # evolving latent positions with AR(1) dynamics U <- matrix(rnorm(n_dyn * R_dyn, 0, 1.5), n_dyn, R_dyn) V <- matrix(rnorm(n_dyn * R_dyn, 0, 1.5), n_dyn, R_dyn)  Y_dyn <- list() for(t in 1:n_per) {     if(t > 1) {         U <- 0.85 * U + matrix(rnorm(n_dyn * R_dyn, 0, 0.3), n_dyn, R_dyn)         V <- 0.85 * V + matrix(rnorm(n_dyn * R_dyn, 0, 0.3), n_dyn, R_dyn)     }     eta <- -1 + U %*% t(V)     Y_t <- matrix(rbinom(n_dyn * n_dyn, 1, pnorm(eta)), n_dyn, n_dyn)     diag(Y_t) <- NA     rownames(Y_t) <- colnames(Y_t) <- paste0("A", 1:n_dyn)     Y_dyn[[t]] <- Y_t }  # note: iterations are reduced for vignette speed. # use burn >= 1000 and nscan >= 5000 for real analyses. fit_dyn_real <- lame(Y_dyn, R = 2,     dynamic_uv = TRUE, dynamic_ab = TRUE,     family = "binary",     burn = 100, nscan = 2500, odens = 25,     verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE)  summary(fit_dyn_real) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> NULL #>  #> Time periods: 5  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: unipartite  #> Dynamic latent positions: enabled (rho_uv = 0.946 ) #> Dynamic additive effects: enabled (rho_ab = 0.403 ) #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept   -0.746    0.162  -4.599       0   -1.004   -0.428 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: p-values are approximate (posterior mean / SD); use credible intervals for inference. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.125    0.085 #> cab    0.061    0.078 #> vb     0.132    0.081 #> rho    0.213    0.116 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) uv_plot(fit_dyn_real, plot_type = "trajectory") trace_plot(fit_dyn_real)

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: understand dynamic model behaves signal, fit model independent networks (temporal correlation). useful null baseline: estimated rho values tell prior alone produces data uninformative. can compare estimated persistence parameters across two settings: 5 time periods, rho_uv estimates may similar cases. expected: AR(1) parameter identified correlation latent positions across adjacent time periods, 5 time points provide little information update prior. real applications 10 time periods, difference genuine dynamics independent data becomes much clearer. key diagnostic trajectory plot (compare coherent paths erratic paths ) rather point estimate rho.
code: set.seed(6886) n <- 30 n_periods <- 5  # independent binary networks with no temporal structure Y_list <- list() for(t in 1:n_periods) {     Y_t <- matrix(rbinom(n*n, 1, 0.2), n, n)     diag(Y_t) <- NA     rownames(Y_t) <- colnames(Y_t) <- paste0("Actor", 1:n)     Y_list[[t]] <- Y_t }  # fit with a custom prior on rho_uv to illustrate prior sensitivity prior_custom <- list(     rho_uv_mean = 0.95,    # expect very slow change in latent positions     rho_uv_sd = 0.05       # tight prior )  fit_null <- lame(     Y = Y_list,     R = 2,     dynamic_uv = TRUE,     dynamic_ab = TRUE,     family = "binary",     prior = prior_custom,     burn = 100,     nscan = 2500,     odens = 25,     verbose = FALSE,     plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_null) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> NULL #>  #> Time periods: 5  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: unipartite  #> Dynamic latent positions: enabled (rho_uv = 0.107 ) #> Dynamic additive effects: enabled (rho_ab = 0.478 ) #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept   -0.863    0.146   -5.92       0   -1.174   -0.608 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: p-values are approximate (posterior mean / SD); use credible intervals for inference. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.076    0.043 #> cab    0.024    0.039 #> vb     0.073    0.044 #> rho    0.196    0.077 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) cat("Independent data rho_uv:", round(mean(fit_null$rho_uv), 3), "\n") #> Independent data rho_uv: 0.107 cat("Correlated data rho_uv:", round(mean(fit_dyn_real$rho_uv), 3), "\n") #> Correlated data rho_uv: 0.946

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: natural workflow fit several specifications compare goodness--fit. use independent data show dynamic effects add nothing signal. Compare GOF statistics across models. specification, compute posterior predictive p-values: often simulated network statistics exceed observed ones? Values near 0.5 indicate good fit. Since data generated without temporal structure, static model fit well better dynamic models. see similar p-values across specifications, confirms dynamic parameters needed .
code: # static model (no dynamic effects) fit_static <- lame(Y_list, R = 2, family = "binary",                                     burn = 100, nscan = 2500, odens = 25,                                     verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE)  # dynamic latent positions only fit_uv <- lame(Y_list, R = 2, dynamic_uv = TRUE, family = "binary",                             burn = 100, nscan = 2500, odens = 25,                             verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE)  # dynamic additive effects only fit_ab <- lame(Y_list, R = 2, dynamic_ab = TRUE, family = "binary",                             burn = 100, nscan = 2500, odens = 25,                             verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE)  # full dynamic fit_full <- lame(Y_list, R = 2, dynamic_uv = TRUE, dynamic_ab = TRUE,                                 family = "binary",                                 burn = 100, nscan = 2500, odens = 25,                                 verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE) # compute p-values for each model and each GOF statistic compute_pvals <- function(fit) {     gof <- fit$GOF     sapply(names(gof), function(stat) {         mat <- gof[[stat]]         obs <- mat[, 1]           # first column = observed         sims <- mat[, -1]         # remaining = posterior predictive         mean(colMeans(sims) >= mean(obs))     }) }  gof_comparison <- rbind(     Static     = compute_pvals(fit_static),     Dynamic_UV = compute_pvals(fit_uv),     Dynamic_AB = compute_pvals(fit_ab),     Full       = compute_pvals(fit_full) ) round(gof_comparison, 3) #>            sd.rowmean sd.colmean dyad.dep cycle.dep trans.dep #> Static           0.90       0.97     0.50      0.57      0.34 #> Dynamic_UV       0.92       0.99     0.51      0.55      0.31 #> Dynamic_AB       0.98       1.00     0.94      0.31      0.34 #> Full             0.87       0.88     0.87      0.39      0.33

title: lame Overview
text: lame package provides tools fitting Longitudinal Additive Multiplicative Effects models network data observed time. study relationships actors (countries trading , legislators co-sponsoring bills, students forming friendships across semesters) repeated observations relationships, lame designed . modeling approach builds Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) framework developed Peter Hoff, whose amen package (GitHub) provides foundational implementation cross-sectional network analysis. lame package extends framework several directions: Longitudinal replicated data: amen focuses single networks (ame()) basic replicated designs (ame_rep()), lame built ground panel data, networks observed across multiple time periods, potentially actors entering leaving sample. Dynamic effects: Actors’ positions latent space baseline activity levels can evolve time via AR(1) processes (dynamic_uv dynamic_ab), letting model network structure changes rather assuming fixed. C++ acceleration: Core sampling routines implemented Rcpp/RcppArmadillo faster computation, matters many time periods actors. Bipartite networks: Explicit support two-mode networks (e.g., countries treaties, users products) separate latent spaces row column nodes. ggplot2-based diagnostics: Built-visualization functions (trace_plot, gof_plot, ab_plot, uv_plot) produce publication-ready ggplot2 figures. Standard S3 methods: coef(), confint(), fitted(), residuals(), predict(), simulate() work expect R model object. package part netify-verse, works seamlessly netify package data preparation.

Entry yij,t=1y_{ij,t} = 1 means country ii imposed sanctions country jj year tt. Xdyad: Dyadic covariates (geographic distance number shared intergovernmental organizations (IGOs)) capture relationship-level predictors. Xrow / Xcol: Sender receiver covariates (log GDP log population) capturing country-level characteristics. use variables senders receivers , different covariates can specified role.

title: lame Overview
text: fit binary probit AME model sender random effects (rvar), receiver random effects (cvar), dyadic correlation (dcor), 2-dimensional latent space (R = 2). random effects capture fact countries simply active sanctioners (sender effects) frequent targets (receiver effects), latent space picks residual patterns, groups countries sanction similar targets targeted senders.
code: # fit the AME model for binary data # note: for real analyses, use burn >= 2000 and nscan >= 10000. # sparse networks like this one require long chains. fit <- lame(     Y = Y,     Xdyad = Xdyad,           # dyadic covariates     Xrow = Xrow,             # sender covariates     Xcol = Xcol,             # receiver covariates     family = "binary",       # Binary probit model     rvar = TRUE,             # sender random effects     cvar = TRUE,             # receiver random effects     dcor = TRUE,             # Dyadic correlation     R = 2,                   # Multiplicative effects dimension     symmetric = FALSE,       # Directed network     burn = 500,              # Burn-in iterations     nscan = 2500,            # Post-burn-in iterations     odens = 5,               # Thinning (keep every 5th sample)     verbose = FALSE,         # Suppress iteration output     plot = FALSE             # Suppress real-time plots )

text: Two essential diagnostics: 1. MCMC converge? trace plots look like fuzzy caterpillars, random walks trends.  intercept-model, just one regression parameter check. trace show stable mixing around value near -1.04 (since qnorm(0.15) corresponds 15% density simulated). 2. model fit data? GOF plots compare observed network statistics model predicts. Observed values fall within simulated distributions.  observed statistics (points vertical lines) fall within posterior predictive bands histograms, model reproducing network’s structural features.

title: Getting Started with lame
text: believe network structure changing time (alliances shifting, friendships evolving), can let latent positions additive effects drift via AR(1) processes: model estimates persistent latent positions (ρuv\rho_{uv}) persistent additive effects (ρab\rho_{ab}). Values near 1 mean slow evolution; values near 0 mean rapid change. Since simulated independent networks, data carry temporal signal. estimated persistence parameters reflect prior rather genuine dynamics. 3 time periods, little information update prior. See dynamic effects vignette thorough treatment 5 time periods discussion dynamic effects add value.
code: fit_dyn <- lame(     Y = Y_list,     R = 2,     dynamic_ab = TRUE,    # time-varying sociality/popularity     dynamic_uv = TRUE,    # time-varying latent positions     family = "binary",     burn = 100,     nscan = 2500,     odens = 25,     verbose = FALSE,     plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_dyn) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> NULL #>  #> Time periods: 3  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: unipartite  #> Dynamic latent positions: enabled (rho_uv = 0.292 ) #> Dynamic additive effects: enabled (rho_ab = 0.301 ) #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept   -1.203    0.249   -4.84       0   -1.661   -0.687 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: p-values are approximate (posterior mean / SD); use credible intervals for inference. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.132    0.074 #> cab    0.056    0.067 #> vb     0.130    0.078 #> rho    0.421    0.124 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance)

title: Getting Started with lame
text: two-mode networks (students courses, countries treaties), pass rectangular matrix set mode = "bipartite": Notice bipartite summary reports cab = 0.000 rho = 0.000 — parameters (sender-receiver covariance dyadic correlation) estimated bipartite models two node types structurally different. See bipartite vignette full walkthrough.
code: nA <- 10; nB <- 8 Y_bip <- lapply(1:3, function(t) {     Y <- matrix(rbinom(nA * nB, 1, 0.2), nA, nB)     rownames(Y) <- paste0("R", 1:nA)     colnames(Y) <- paste0("C", 1:nB)     Y })  fit_bip <- lame(     Y = Y_bip,     mode = "bipartite",     R = 2,     family = "binary",     burn = 100, nscan = 2500, odens = 25,     verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_bip) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> NULL #>  #> Time periods: 3  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: bipartite  #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper    #> intercept   -1.176    0.428  -2.745   0.006   -1.894   -0.375 ** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: p-values are approximate (posterior mean / SD); use credible intervals for inference. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.682    0.264 #> cab    0.000    0.000 #> vb     0.666    0.270 #> rho    0.000    0.000 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) #>   Note: bipartite model (rho fixed to 0, cab fixed to 0)

what: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: Longitudinal Additive Multiplicative Effects Models Networks lame package builds extends Peter Hoff’s amen package, implements Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) models cross-sectional network analysis. amen provides foundational Bayesian framework modeling network data latent factor nodal random effects, lame extends several directions: (1) longitudinal network analysis via lame() dynamic additive multiplicative effects modeled AR(1) processes, (2) support bipartite (rectangular) network structures, (3) C++ acceleration core MCMC sampling routines via Rcpp RcppArmadillo, (4) full suite S3 methods (coef(), vcov(), confint(), predict(), fitted(), residuals(), simulate()) standard R model interoperability. package includes two main functions: ame() cross-sectional network analysis lame() longitudinal network analysis dynamic effects capture temporal heterogeneity autoregressive processes. functions support unipartite (square) bipartite (rectangular) network structures.

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: Install GitHub (requires C++ build tools: Xcode CLI macOS, Rtools Windows, build-essential Linux):
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("netify-dev/lame", dependencies = TRUE)

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
code: library(lame)  # Load example data (creates Y, Xdyad, Xrow, Xcol in your workspace) data("vignette_data")  # Y      — list of 4 binary network matrices (35 × 35, years 1993–2000) # Xdyad  — list of dyadic covariate arrays (distance, shared IGOs) # Xrow   — list of sender covariate matrices (GDP, population) # Xcol   — list of receiver covariate matrices (GDP, population)  # Fit a basic longitudinal AME model fit <- lame(   Y = Y,                    # List of T network matrices   Xdyad = Xdyad,           # Dyadic covariates   Xrow = Xrow,             # Sender covariates   Xcol = Xcol,             # Receiver covariates   family = "binary",       # Network type   R = 2,                   # Latent dimensions   burn = 100,              # Burn-in iterations   nscan = 500              # Post-burn samples )  # Summary and diagnostics summary(fit)

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: key innovation lame time-varying network effects:
code: # Fit model with dynamic effects (builds on fit and data from Quick Start above) fit_dynamic <- lame(   Y = Y,   Xdyad = Xdyad,   Xrow = Xrow,   Xcol = Xcol,   family = "binary",   dynamic_ab = TRUE,       # Time-varying sender/receiver effects   dynamic_uv = TRUE,       # Time-varying latent positions   R = 2,   burn = 100,   nscan = 500,   prior = list(     rho_uv_mean = 0.9,    # High persistence for latent factors     rho_ab_mean = 0.8     # Moderate persistence for additive effects   ) )

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: MCMC routine providing fit additive multiplicative effects (AME) regression model cross-sectional relational data various types. function supports unipartite (square) bipartite (rectangular) networks. longitudinal networks, use lame function. Original implementation Peter Hoff.

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
code: ame(Y, Xdyad=NULL, Xrow=NULL, Xcol=NULL, rvar = !(family=="rrl") , cvar = TRUE,  dcor = !symmetric, nvar=TRUE, R = 0, R_row = NULL, R_col = NULL, mode = c("unipartite", "bipartite"), family="normal", intercept=!is.element(family,c("rrl","ordinal")), symmetric=FALSE, odmax=rep(max(apply(Y>0,1,sum,na.rm=TRUE)),nrow(Y)), prior=list(), g=NA, seed = 6886, nscan = 10000, burn = 500, odens = 25, verbose = TRUE, gof=TRUE, custom_gof=NULL, start_vals=NULL, periodic_save=FALSE, out_file=NULL, save_interval=0.25, model.name=NULL, posterior_opts = NULL, n_chains = 1, cores = 1, use_sparse_matrices = FALSE, print)

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: Y unipartite: n x n square relational matrix. bipartite: nA x nB rectangular relational matrix nA number row nodes nB number column nodes. See family various data types. Xdyad unipartite: n x n x pd array covariates. bipartite: nA x nB x pd array covariates. Xrow unipartite: n x pr matrix nodal row covariates. bipartite: nA x pr matrix row node covariates. Xcol unipartite: n x pc matrix nodal column covariates. bipartite: nB x pc matrix column node covariates. rvar logical: fit row random effects (asymmetric case)? cvar logical: fit column random effects (asymmetric case)? dcor logical: fit dyadic correlation (asymmetric case)? Note: used bipartite networks. nvar logical: fit nodal random effects (symmetric case)? R integer: dimension multiplicative effects (can zero). bipartite networks, used default R_row R_col specified. R_row integer: bipartite networks, dimension row node multiplicative effects (defaults R) R_col integer: bipartite networks, dimension column node multiplicative effects (defaults R) mode character: either "unipartite" (default) square networks "bipartite" rectangular networks family character: one "normal","tobit","binary","ordinal","cbin","frn","rrl","poisson" - see details intercept logical: fit model intercept? symmetric logical: sociomatrix symmetric design? odmax scalar integer vector length n giving maximum number nominations node may make - used "frn" "cbin" families prior list containing hyperparameters prior distributions. Available options defaults: Sab0 Prior covariance matrix additive effects (default: diag(2)). 2x2 matrix Sab0\[1,1\] prior variance row effects, Sab0\[2,2\] prior variance column effects, -diagonals control correlation row column effects. eta0 Prior degrees freedom covariance multiplicative effects (default: 4 + 3 \* n/100, n number actors). Higher values impose stronger shrinkage latent factors. Common values: 4-10 weak shrinkage, 10-20 moderate, >20 strong shrinkage. etaab Prior degrees freedom covariance additive effects (default: 4 + 3 \* n/100). Controls shrinkage row/column random effects. Larger values shrink effects toward zero. s20 Prior variance regression coefficients (default: 1). Larger values allow larger coefficient values. s2u0 Prior variance multiplicative effects (default: 1). Suv0 Prior covariance multiplicative effects (default: identity matrix). Common usage: prior = list(Sab0 = diag(c(2, 2)), eta0 = 10) moderate shrinkage, prior = list(Sab0 = diag(c(0.5, 0.5))) tighter control. g optional scalar vector length dim(X)\[3\] g-prior regression coefficients. specified, defaults : normal family, g = nvar(Y); tobit, g = nvar(Y)*4; families, g = n, n number non-missing dyads. g-prior controls variance regression coefficients. seed random seed nscan number iterations Markov chain (beyond burn-) burn burn Markov chain odens output density Markov chain verbose logical: print progress running? Default TRUE. gof logical: calculate goodness fit statistics? Setting TRUE adds approximately 2-5% runtime. faster sampling without GOF overhead, set gof=FALSE use gof() model fitting. custom_gof optional function list named functions computing custom goodness--fit statistics. function must accept single matrix Y input return numeric vector. single function provided, return named vector. list functions provided, function return single value named according list names. Custom statistics computed addition default statistics. Example: custom_gof = function(Y) c(density = mean(Y > 0, na.rm = TRUE)) start_vals List previous model run containing parameter starting values new MCMC periodic_save logical: indicating whether periodically save MCMC results out_file character vector indicating name path file stored periodic_save selected. example, Apple OS out_file="~/Desktop/ameFit.rda". save_interval quantile interval indicating save post burn-phase. model.name optional string model selection output posterior_opts optional list posterior sampling options n_chains integer: number MCMC chains run (default: 1) cores integer: number cores parallel chains (default: 1) use_sparse_matrices logical: use sparse matrix storage large networks? (default: FALSE). Recommended truly sparse networks (< 10% non-zero entries). print Deprecated. Use verbose instead.

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: Posterior Samples (full MCMC chains): BETA Regression coefficients (nscan xp matrix) VC Variance components (nscan xk matrix) GOF Goodness--fit statistics (nscan x4 matrix). First row contains observed values, remaining rows contain posterior predictive samples. See gof post-hoc computation gof_plot visualization. Posterior Means (averaged chain): APM Additive row/sender effects (n-vector) BPM Additive column/receiver effects (m-vector); NULL symmetric networks U Multiplicative row/sender factors (n xR matrix) V Multiplicative column/receiver factors (m xR matrix); NULL symmetric networks L Eigenvalue matrix (R xR diagonal); symmetric networks YPM Posterior mean Y response scale (predictions imputing missing values) Metadata: family Model family (normal, binary, etc.) mode Network mode (unipartite bipartite) symmetric Logical indicating network symmetric R Dimension multiplicative effects Optional Posterior Samples (requested via posterior_options): U_samples Samples U (n xR xiterations array) V_samples Samples V (m xR xiterations array) a_samples Samples row effects (n xiterations matrix) b_samples Samples column effects (m xiterations matrix) Note reconstructing removed matrices: save memory, EZ (expected latent network) UVPM/ULUPM (multiplicative products) stored can reconstructed using: reconstruct_EZ(fit) - Returns linear predictor (link scale, response scale) reconstruct_UVPM(fit) - Returns U\ Generating posterior distributions: Use simulate_posterior(fit, component="UV") generate posterior samples components means stored, use posterior_options() model fitting save full posterior samples. model.name Name model (provided)

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: command provides posterior inference parameters AME models cross-sectional relational data, assuming one eight possible data types/models. function supports unipartite networks (square adjacency matrices) bipartite networks (rectangular adjacency matrices distinct row column node sets) single time point analysis. Theoretical Foundation: AME model decomposes network structure several components: $$y_{ij} = \beta'x_{ij} + a_i + b_j + u_i'v_j + \epsilon_{ij}$$ : \(\beta'x_{ij}\): Fixed effects dyadic/nodal covariates \(a_i\): Additive sender (row) effect node \(b_j\): Additive receiver (column) effect node j \(u_i'v_j\): Multiplicative interaction latent factors \(\epsilon_{ij}\): Dyadic error term (may correlated) specification generalizes social relations model (Warner et al. 1979) latent space models (Hoff et al. 2002) within unified framework. Prior Distributions: model uses conjugate semi-conjugate priors possible: Regression coefficients: \(\beta \sim N(0, g\sigma^2(X'X)^{-1})\) (g-prior) Additive effects: \((a_i, b_i)' \sim N(0, \Sigma_{ab})\) jointly Covariance: \(\Sigma_{ab} \sim IW(\eta_0, \eta_0 S_{ab0})\) (inverse-Wishart) Multiplicative effects: Hierarchical shrinkage via \(\eta_0\) Dyadic correlation: \(\rho \sim Uniform(-1, 1)\) Metropolis updates inverse-Wishart prior \(\Sigma_{ab}\) allows learning correlation sender receiver effects, capturing reciprocity patterns. Multiplicative Effects (Latent Factors): R > 0, model includes R-dimensional latent factors: Asymmetric case: \(u_i, v_j \\mathbb{R}^R\) \(u_i'v_j\) interaction Symmetric case: \(u_i = v_i\) eigendecomposition \(ULU'\) Captures homophily, transitivity, community structure R chosen via model selection set 2-3 visualization Estimation Algorithm: model uses Gibbs sampler following updates: Sample latent Z given parameters (data augmentation non-normal families) Update regression coefficients \(\beta\) via g-prior conjugate update Update additive effects (,b) jointly \(\beta\) Update covariance \(\Sigma_{ab}\) inverse-Wishart Update multiplicative effects U,V via Gibbs Metropolis-Hastings Update dyadic correlation \(\rho\) via Metropolis-Hastings Update variance \(\sigma^2\) (continuous families) Standard Model Types: following data types/models available: "normal": normal AME model (identity link: \(E[Y] = \eta\)). "tobit": tobit AME model censored continuous data. Values censored zero, appropriate non-negative continuous relational data (identity link censoring). "binary": binary probit AME model (probit link: \(P(Y=1) = \Phi(\eta)\)). "ordinal": ordinal probit AME model (cumulative probit link). intercept identifiable model. "cbin": AME model censored binary data (probit link censoring). value 'odmax' specifies maximum number links row may . "frn": AME model fixed rank nomination networks. higher value rank indicates stronger relationship. value 'odmax' specifies maximum number links row may . "rrl": AME model based row ranks. appropriate relationships across rows directly comparable terms scale. intercept, row random effects row regression effects estimable model. "poisson": overdispersed Poisson AME model count data (log link: \(E[Y] = \exp(\eta)\)). linear predictor \(\eta\) represents \(\log(\lambda)\) \(\lambda\) expected count.

title: Visualize goodness-of-fit statistics for AME and LAME models — gof_plot
text: Overview: Goodness--fit (GOF) assessment crucial network models standard residual diagnostics often inadequate capturing network dependencies. function implements posterior predictive checking : Computing key network statistics observed data Generating multiple networks model's posterior predictive distribution Computing statistics simulated networks Visualizing comparison identify model inadequacies Network Statistics Evaluated: unipartite (square) networks: sd.row (-degree heterogeneity) Standard deviation row means. High values indicate substantial variation active nodes senders/initiators. model underestimates , may missing important sender effects covariates. sd.col (-degree heterogeneity) Standard deviation column means. High values indicate substantial variation node popularity receivers. Underestimation suggests missing receiver effects popularity-related covariates. dyad.dep (Reciprocity/Mutuality) Correlation Y[,j] Y[j,]. Positive values indicate reciprocity (mutual ties likely). AME model captures dyadic correlation parameter rho. Poor fit suggests need adjust dcor parameter. triad.dep (Transitivity/Clustering) Measures tendency triadic closure (friend friend friend). Calculated correlation Y[,j] sum(Y[,k]*Y[k,j])/sqrt(n-2). AME captures multiplicative effects (U,V). Poor fit suggests increasing latent dimension R. bipartite (rectangular) networks: sd.row (Type activity variation) Standard deviation row means Type nodes. Indicates heterogeneity actively Type nodes connect Type B nodes. sd.col (Type B popularity variation) Standard deviation column means Type B nodes. Indicates heterogeneity popular Type B nodes Type nodes. four.cycles (Bipartite clustering) Count 4-cycles (rectangular paths A1-B1-A2-B2-A1). High values indicate pairs Type nodes tend connect Type B nodes. Captured bipartite multiplicative effects appropriate R_row, R_col. Interpretation Guide: Histogram plots (static models): Red vertical line: observed statistic value Blue histogram: distribution posterior predictive simulations Good fit: red line falls within bulk histogram Poor fit: red line tail outside distribution Common model inadequacies solutions: Observed sd.row/sd.col high Model underestimates degree heterogeneity. Solutions: Add row/column covariates (Xrow, Xcol), enable random effects (rvar=TRUE, cvar=TRUE), increase variance. Observed dyad.dep outside distribution Reciprocity captured well. Solutions: positive reciprocity, ensure dcor=TRUE. negative, consider transformation different family. Observed triad.dep high Clustering/transitivity underestimated. Solutions: Increase latent dimension R, add network covariates capture homophily, consider including community structure covariates. Multiple statistics showing poor fit Fundamental model misspecification. Solutions: Change family, add missing covariates, consider different model class. Mathematical Details: posterior predictive p-value statistic s : $$p = P(s(Y_{rep}) \geq s(Y_{obs}) | Y_{obs})$$ Y_rep drawn posterior predictive distribution. Values near 0 1 indicate poor fit. function visualizes full distribution rather just p-values richer diagnostics. Customization: function accepts custom GOF statistics model's custom_gof argument. automatically included plot. Custom statistics capture network features important specific application. Computational Notes: GOF computation involves simulating multiple networks, can computationally intensive. model uses networks simulated MCMC gof=TRUE specified. post-hoc GOF, use gof() function generates new simulations. Good model fit indicated : Observed statistics fall within central 95% posterior predictive distributions systematic patterns /underestimation across statistics Custom statistics (provided) also show adequate fit longitudinal models, observed trajectories track within credible bands

what: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
text: extension 'amen' package specifically designed longitudinal network analysis. package provides tools fitting Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) models longitudinal/replicated relational data several key enhancements: (1) robust handling changing actor compositions across time periods, allowing networks different sets actors time point; (2) significant performance improvements C++ implementations via Rcpp RcppArmadillo; (3) specialized functions temporal network dynamics. package supports eight data types: normal (nrm), binary (bin), ordinal (ord), Poisson count (poisson), tobit/censored continuous (tobit), censored binary (cbin), fixed-rank nomination (frn), row-ranked (rrl). Based AME framework originally developed Hoff (2009) Hoff, Fosdick, Volfovsky Stovel (2013).

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
text: Shahryar Minhas, Tosin Salau, Cassy Dorff Maintainer: Shahryar Minhas minhassh@msu.edu

title: AME model fitting routine for longitudinal relational data — lame
text: command provides posterior inference parameters AME models longitudinal relational data, assuming one eight possible data types/models. model supports unipartite networks (square adjacency matrices) bipartite networks (rectangular adjacency matrices distinct row column node sets) across multiple time points. Dynamic Effects Implementation: dynamic_uv dynamic_ab parameters enable time-varying latent representations autoregressive processes. extensions particularly useful understanding network structure evolves time. Dynamic Multiplicative Effects (dynamic_uv=TRUE): latent factors U V evolve according AR(1) processes: $$U_{,k,t} = \rho_{uv} U_{,k,t-1} + \epsilon_{,k,t}$$ \(\epsilon_{,k,t} \sim N(0, \sigma_{uv}^2)\), indexes actors, k indexes latent dimensions, t indexes time. parameter \(\rho_{uv}\) controls temporal persistence (values near 1 indicate slow evolution). captures time-varying homophily, latent community structure, transitivity dynamics. Key references: Sewell & Chen (2015): Introduced dynamic latent space models actor-specific evolution rates Durante & Dunson (2014): Nonparametric Bayesian approach allowing flexible evolution network structure Hoff (2011): Hierarchical multilinear models providing theoretical foundation temporal dependencies Dynamic Additive Effects (dynamic_ab=TRUE): sender () receiver (b) effects evolve : $$a_{,t} = \rho_{ab} a_{,t-1} + \epsilon_{,t}$$ $$b_{,t} = \rho_{ab} b_{,t-1} + \eta_{,t}$$ \(\epsilon_{,t}, \eta_{,t} \sim N(0, \sigma_{ab}^2)\). models time-varying individual activity levels (outdegree) popularity (indegree). Applications include: Tracking changes node centrality time Identifying emerging influential actors Detecting declining activity patterns Modeling life-cycle effects social networks Prior Specification Dynamic Parameters: \(\rho_{uv}, \rho_{ab} \sim TruncNormal(mean, sd, 0, 1)\): Ensures stationarity AR(1) process \(\sigma_{uv}^2, \sigma_{ab}^2 \sim InverseGamma(shape, scale)\): Controls innovation variance Default priors (\(\rho_{uv}\) mean=0.9, \(\rho_{ab}\) mean=0.8) favor smooth evolution Adjust rho_*_mean closer 1 slower evolution, closer 0 rapid changes Computational Considerations: Dynamic effects increase computation ~30-50\ Memory usage scales O(nRT) dynamic_uv, O(n*T) dynamic_ab C++ implementation provides ~70\ Convergence diagnostics: Monitor rho sigma parameters carefully Effective sample sizes typically lower due temporal correlation Recommend burn >= 1000 nscan >= 20000 dynamic models Model Selection Guidelines: Use dynamic_uv dynamic_ab : Networks show clear temporal trends density clustering Individual node behavior changes systematically time Community structure evolves (merging, splitting, drift) Use dynamic_uv : Latent structure/communities change individual effects stable Focus evolving homophily clustering patterns Network shows structural reconfiguration time Use dynamic_ab : Individual heterogeneity varies overall structure stable Actors' activity/popularity changes observation period Focus individual-level temporal dynamics Bipartite Network Models: mode="bipartite", model handles rectangular adjacency matrices Y dimensions n_A x n_B, n_A n_B represent number row column nodes respectively. Static Bipartite Case: model uses separate latent factor matrices: U: n_A x R_row matrix row node latent positions V: n_B x R_col matrix column node latent positions G: R_row x R_col interaction matrix mapping latent spaces Multiplicative term: U G V' captures bipartite community structure Dynamic Bipartite Case: dynamic_uv=TRUE bipartite networks: $$U_{,k,t} = \rho_{uv} U_{,k,t-1} + \epsilon_{,k,t}$$ $$V_{j,k,t} = \rho_{uv} V_{j,k,t-1} + \eta_{j,k,t}$$ indexes row nodes, j indexes column nodes, k indexes latent dimensions. Note: dynamic_G reserved future use yet implemented. Key Differences Unipartite Models: dyadic correlation (rho): Bipartite edges inherently directed Separate dimensions: R_row R_col can differ row/column spaces Rectangular structure: Network density patterns differ square matrices Community interpretation: Captures affiliation patterns node types Standard AME Model Types: following describes eight standard data types/models available: "normal": normal AME model. "tobit": tobit AME model censored continuous data. Values censored zero, appropriate non-negative continuous relational data. "binary": binary probit AME model. "ordinal": ordinal probit AME model. intercept identifiable model. "cbin": AME model censored binary data.  value 'odmax' specifies maximum number links row may . "frn": AME model fixed rank nomination networks. higher value rank indicates stronger relationship. value 'odmax' specifies maximum number links row may . "rrl": AME model based row ranks. appropriate relationships across rows directly comparable terms scale. intercept, row random effects row regression effects estimable model. "poisson": overdispersed Poisson AME model count data. latent variable represents log mean Poisson distribution.

what: Procrustes alignment of latent positions across time — procrustes_align
title: Procrustes alignment of latent positions across time — procrustes_align
text: Aligns dynamic latent positions across time periods remove arbitrary rotational indeterminacy. essential interpreting temporal trajectories latent positions, since latent space identified rotation time point. Uses Procrustes rotation: time step t, finds orthogonal rotation matrix best aligns U_t U_t-1 (minimizing Frobenius norm), applies sequentially t=2 T.

title: Procrustes alignment of latent positions across time — procrustes_align
code: procrustes_align(   object = NULL,   U = NULL,   V = NULL,   G = NULL,   return_fit = FALSE,   ... )

title: Procrustes alignment of latent positions across time — procrustes_align
text: object fitted ame lame object, NULL raw arrays provided via U/V/G. U Optional 3D array [n, R, T] sender latent positions. object provided U NULL, extracted object$U. V Optional 3D array [n, R, T] receiver latent positions. unipartite asymmetric models, aligned independently. bipartite models, aligned jointly U via G interaction matrix. G Optional [R_row, R_col] interaction matrix bipartite models. Updated maintain invariant U G V'. return_fit Logical. TRUE object provided, returns modified copy fit object aligned latent positions. Default FALSE. ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Procrustes alignment of latent positions across time — procrustes_align
text: return_fit = FALSE (default): list components U (aligned sender positions), V (aligned receiver positions, applicable), G (updated interaction matrix, bipartite). return_fit = TRUE: copy object aligned latent positions replacing originals.

title: Procrustes alignment of latent positions across time — procrustes_align
text: unipartite networks, U V aligned independently using separate Procrustes rotations. symmetric networks, U present aligned. bipartite networks, U V aligned jointly: separate rotation matrices computed U V, G interaction matrix updated G_aligned = t(R_U) %*% G %*% R_V preserve product U %*% G %*% t(V). U 2D matrix (static model single time point), returned unchanged informational message.

title: Procrustes alignment of latent positions across time — procrustes_align
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas, Tosin Salau

text: vector row random effects (additive sender effects) b vector column random effects (additive receiver effects) Sab0 prior (inverse) scale matrix prior distribution. title: Gibbs update for additive effects covariance — rSab_fc
text: Updated covariance matrix Sab (2x2 matrix variances diagonal covariance -diagonal)

title: Gibbs update for additive effects covariance — rSab_fc
text: function implements different update strategies: Full update: rvar cvar TRUE, updates full 2x2 covariance matrix using inverse-Wishart distribution Row variance : rvar TRUE, updates Sab[1,1] Column variance : cvar TRUE, updates Sab[2,2] Symmetric case: symmetric TRUE, enforces equal variances high correlation (0.999) row column effects

title: Gibbs update for additive effects covariance — rSab_fc
text: Peter Hoff, Shahryar Minhas

what: Gibbs update for multiplicative effects covariance — rSuv_fc

text: Gibbs sampling covariance matrix multiplicative effects U V AME model. function implements inverse-Wishart posterior update covariance matrix stacked UV effects.

title: Gibbs update for multiplicative effects covariance — rSuv_fc
code: rSuv_fc(U, V, Suv0=NULL, kappa0=NULL)

title: Gibbs update for multiplicative effects covariance — rSuv_fc
text: U matrix multiplicative row effects (n x R matrix n number nodes R dimension multiplicative effects) V matrix multiplicative column effects (n x R matrix) Suv0 prior (inverse) scale matrix prior distribution. Default identity matrix dimension 2R x 2R, providing weakly informative prior. kappa0 prior degrees freedom prior distribution. Default 2 + 2R, minimum proper prior 2R x 2R covariance matrix.

title: Gibbs update for multiplicative effects covariance — rSuv_fc
text: Updated covariance matrix Suv (2R x 2R matrix) stacked effects [U, V]. first R x R block contains covariances U, last R x R block contains covariances V, -diagonal blocks contain cross-covariances U V.

title: Gibbs update for multiplicative effects covariance — rSuv_fc
text: function updates full covariance matrix multiplicative effects using inverse-Wishart distribution. posterior distribution : $$Suv ~ IW(kappa0 * Suv0 + t(UV) \%*\% UV, n + kappa0)$$ UV = cbind(U, V) stacked matrix effects. hierarchical prior allows adaptive shrinkage multiplicative effects, amount shrinkage determined data posterior update.

title: Gibbs update for multiplicative effects covariance — rSuv_fc
text: Peter Hoff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Simulate networks from a fitted AME model — simulate.ame
text: Mathematical Framework: AME model represents networks latent variable framework: $$Y_{ij} \sim F(Z_{ij})$$ F observation model (e.g., probit binary) Z latent network: $$Z_{ij} = \beta^T x_{ij} + a_i + b_j + u_i^T v_j + \epsilon_{ij}$$ Components: \(\beta\): regression coefficients dyadic/nodal covariates \(a_i, b_j\): additive sender receiver random effects \(u_i, v_j\): multiplicative latent factors (dimension R) \(\epsilon_{ij}\): dyadic random effects correlation \(\rho\) Uncertainty Quantification Process: simulated network k = 1, ..., nsim: Parameter Sampling: Draw parameter set \(\theta^{(k)}\) MCMC chains: Sample iteration s uniformly stored MCMC samples Extract \(\beta^{(s)}\), variance components \((v_a^{(s)}, v_b^{(s)}, v_e^{(s)}, \rho^{(s)})\) Random Effects Generation: Sample new random effects posterior distributions: \(a_i^{(k)} \sim N(0, v_a^{(s)})\) = 1, ..., n (row effects) \(b_j^{(k)} \sim N(0, v_b^{(s)})\) j = 1, ..., m (column effects) Note: sample fresh posterior variance rather using point estimates properly propagate uncertainty Latent Network Construction: Build expected latent positions: $$E[Z_{ij}^{(k)}] = \beta^{(s)T} x_{ij} + a_i^{(k)} + b_j^{(k)} + \hat{u}_i^T \hat{v}_j$$ \(\hat{u}_i, \hat{v}_j\) posterior mean latent factors Dyadic Correlation: Add correlated noise structure: $$Z_{ij}^{(k)} = E[Z_{ij}^{(k)}] + \epsilon_{ij}^{(k)}$$ \(\epsilon\) covariance structure: $$Cov(\epsilon_{ij}, \epsilon_{ji}) = \rho^{(s)} v_e^{(s)}$$ $$Var(\epsilon_{ij}) = v_e^{(s)}$$ Observation Model: Generate observed network based family: Binary: \(Y_{ij}^{(k)} = (Z_{ij}^{(k)} > 0)\) Normal: \(Y_{ij}^{(k)} = Z_{ij}^{(k)}\) Poisson: \(Y_{ij}^{(k)} \sim Poisson(\exp(Z_{ij}^{(k)}))\) families use appropriate link functions Sources Uncertainty: simulation captures three types uncertainty: Parameter uncertainty: Different MCMC samples yield different \(\beta, v_a, v_b, v_e, \rho\) Random effect uncertainty: Fresh draws \(N(0, v_a), N(0, v_b)\) simulation Dyadic uncertainty: Correlated random noise \(\epsilon_{ij}\) approach provides proper posterior predictive distributions account sources uncertainty model. variation across simulated networks reflects posterior uncertainty data generating process. Limitations: Currently, multiplicative effects (U, V) use posterior means rather sampling full posterior. complete uncertainty quantification, one need store sample full MCMC chains latent factors, require substantial additional memory. Symmetric Networks: symmetric networks, model enforces \(a_i = b_i\) \(u_i = v_i\), latent matrix Z symmetrized generating observations.

text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas, Tosin Salau
